content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Hello - Polite'? {{c1::안녕하세요 (Annyeong-haseyo)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hello - Polite' en Korean? 안녕하세요 (Annyeong-haseyo)
content.usage: Term: 안녕하세요 (Annyeong-haseyo) | Meaning: Hello - Polite

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Hi / Bye - Informal'? {{c1::안녕 (Annyeong)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Hi / Bye - Informal' en Korean? 안녕 (Annyeong)
content.usage: Term: 안녕 (Annyeong) | Meaning: Hi / Bye - Informal

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Nice to meet you'? {{c1::만나서 반갑습니다 (Mannaseo bangapseumnida)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Nice to meet you' en Korean? 만나서 반갑습니다 (Mannaseo bangapseumnida)
content.usage: Term: 만나서 반갑습니다 (Mannaseo bangapseumnida) | Meaning: Nice to meet you

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Goodbye - To person staying'? {{c1::안녕히 계세요 (Annyeonghi gyeseyo)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye - To person staying' en Korean? 안녕히 계세요 (Annyeonghi gyeseyo)
content.usage: Term: 안녕히 계세요 (Annyeonghi gyeseyo) | Meaning: Goodbye - To person staying

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Goodbye - To person leaving'? {{c1::안녕히 가세요 (Annyeonghi gaseyo)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Goodbye - To person leaving' en Korean? 안녕히 가세요 (Annyeonghi gaseyo)
content.usage: Term: 안녕히 가세요 (Annyeonghi gaseyo) | Meaning: Goodbye - To person leaving

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Good night - Polite'? {{c1::잘 자요 (Jal jayo)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Good night - Polite' en Korean? 잘 자요 (Jal jayo)
content.usage: Term: 잘 자요 (Jal jayo) | Meaning: Good night - Polite

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Did you eat? / Hello'? {{c1::밥 먹었어? (Bap meogeosseo?)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Did you eat? / Hello' en Korean? 밥 먹었어? (Bap meogeosseo?)
content.usage: Term: 밥 먹었어? (Bap meogeosseo?) | Meaning: Did you eat? / Hello

content.text: In Korean, the standard polite greeting 'Hello' is '{{c1::안녕하세요}}' (Annyeonghaseyo).
content.explanation: This greeting is in the polite 'haeyo' style. Informally, among close friends, you say '안녕' (Annyeong).
content.spanish: En coreano, el saludo estándar 'Hola' en tono cortés es '안녕하세요' (Annyeonghaseyo).
content.usage: Polite: 안녕하세요 | Informal: 안녕

content.text: In Korean, if you are LEAVING and the other person is STAYING, you say: '{{c1::안녕히 계세요}}' (Annyeonghi gyeseyo).
content.explanation: Literally: 'Please stay peacefully.' '계시다' (gyesida) is the honorific verb for 'to stay'.
content.spanish: En coreano, si te VAS y la otra persona se QUEDA, dices: '안녕히 계세요' (Annyeonghi gyeseyo).
content.usage: Term: 안녕히 계세요

content.text: In Korean, if you are STAYING (or both of you are leaving) and the other person is GOING, you say: '{{c1::안녕히 가세요}}' (Annyeonghi gaseyo).
content.explanation: Literally: 'Please go peacefully.' '가다' (gada) is the verb 'to go'.
content.spanish: En coreano, si te QUEDAS y la otra persona se VA, dices: '안녕히 가세요' (Annyeonghi gaseyo).
content.usage: Term: 안녕히 가세요

content.text: In Korean, the standard polite way to say 'Thank you' is '{{c1::감사합니다}}' (Gamsahabnida) or '{{c2::고맙습니다}}' (Gomapseubnida).
content.explanation: Both are polite formal (하십시오체) endings. 'Gamsahabnida' is Sino-Korean and slightly more formal.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Gracias' formalmente en coreano? 감사합니다 (Gamsahabnida) / 고맙습니다 (Gomapseubnida).
content.usage: Polite: 감사합니다 | Casual: 고마워 (Thanks)

content.text: In Korean, the polite way to say 'Sorry / I apologize' is '{{c1::죄송합니다}}' (Joesonghabnida) or '{{c2::미안합니다}}' (Mianhabnida).
content.explanation: 'Joesonghabnida' is higher respect and used with superiors/strangers.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Lo siento' formalmente en coreano? 죄송합니다 (Joesonghabnida) / 미안합니다 (Mianhabnida).
content.usage: Polite: 죄송합니다 | Casual: 미안해 (Sorry)

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Nice to meet you' at a first meeting? {{c1::반갑습니다}} (Bangapseubnida) or {{c2::처음 뵙겠습니다}} (Cheoeum boepgessseubnida).
content.explanation: 'Cheoeum boepgessseubnida' is very formal (lit. 'I meet you for the first time'). 'Bangapseubnida' is standard polite.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mucho gusto / Encantado' en coreano? 반갑습니다 (Bangapseubnida) / 처음 뵙겠습니다.
content.usage: Dialogue: - 반갑습니다. - 네, 반갑습니다.

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'My name is [Name]' politely? '제 이름은 [Name]입니다' ({{c1::Je ireumeun [Name]-ibnida}}).
content.explanation: '제' (je) is polite 'my', '이름' (ireum) is 'name', and '입니다' (ibnida) is 'is'.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Mi nombre es...' en coreano? 제 이름은 [Nombre]입니다 (Je ireumeun... ibnida).
content.usage: Example: 제 이름은 지저스입니다. (My name is Jesus.)

content.text: In Korean, how do you ask 'What is your name?' politely? {{c1::이름이 무엇입니까?}} (Ireumi mueos-ibnikka?) or {{c2::성함이 어떻게 되세요?}} (Sunghami eotteohge doeseyo? - highly respectful).
content.explanation: '성함' (sungham) is the honorific word for name.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo te llamas / cuál es su nombre?' en coreano? 이름이 무엇입니까? / 성함이 어떻게 되세요?.
content.usage: Polite: 이름이 무엇입니까? | Respectful: 성함이 어떻게 되세요?

content.text: In Korean, a common polite suffix added to names to show respect (like 'Mr./Ms.') is '{{c1::씨}}' (ssi). Example: Marco = 마르코{{c2::씨}}.
content.explanation: Added to the full name or just the given name. Never added to the family name alone.
content.spanish: En coreano, el sufijo honorífico equivalente a 'Señor/Señorita' que se añade al nombre es '씨' (ssi).
content.usage: Example: 마르코 씨, 안녕하세요? (Hello, Mr. Marco.)

content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'Please' (when requesting something)? {{c1::주세요}} (Juseyo) or {{c2::부탁드립니다}} (Butagdeuribnida).
content.explanation: '주세요' is literally 'please give me'. 'Butagdeuribnida' means 'I request of you'.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Por favor / Deme' en coreano? 주세요 (Juseyo) / 부탁드립니다 (Butagdeuribnida).
content.usage: Examples: 물 주세요. (Water, please.) | 도와주세요. (Help me, please.)

content.text: In Korean, a polite way to say 'You're welcome' is '{{c1::천만에요}}' (Cheonmaneyo) or simply '{{c2::아니에요}}' (Anieyo - 'No problem / It's nothing').
content.explanation: 'Cheonmaneyo' is textbook but rarely used in speech. 'Anieyo' is much more natural.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'De nada' en coreano? 천만에요 (Cheonmaneyo) / 아니에요 (Anieyo - más natural).
content.usage: Dialogue: - 감사합니다! - 아니에요.

content.text: In Korean, how do you ask 'How have you been / How are you?' politely? {{c1::어떻게 지내세요?}} (Eotteohge jinaeseyo?)
content.explanation: Literally: 'How are you spending time?'.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice '¿Cómo estás / Cómo te va?' en coreano? 어떻게 지내세요? (Eotteohge jinaeseyo?).
content.usage: Dialogue: - 어떻게 지내세요? - 잘 지내요. (I'm doing well.)

content.text: In Korean, what is the polite phrase meaning 'Please look after me / I look forward to working with you'? {{c1::잘 부탁드립니다}} (Jal butagdeuribnida).
content.explanation: A crucial cultural phrase in self-introductions in business, school, or community.
content.spanish: En coreano, la frase típica de cortesía al presentarse que significa 'Por favor, cuide de mí' es '잘 부탁드립니다'.
content.usage: Term: 잘 부탁드립니다

content.scenario: Korean A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'To be'? {{c1::이다 (ida)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be' en Korean? 이다 (ida)
content.usage: Term: 이다 (ida) | Meaning: To be

content.scenario: Korean A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'To be - Formal'? {{c1::입니다 (imnida)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be - Formal' en Korean? 입니다 (imnida)
content.usage: Term: 입니다 (imnida) | Meaning: To be - Formal

content.scenario: Korean A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'To be - Polite'? {{c1::예요 / 이에요 (yeyo / ieyo)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be - Polite' en Korean? 예요 / 이에요 (yeyo / ieyo)
content.usage: Term: 예요 / 이에요 (yeyo / ieyo) | Meaning: To be - Polite

content.scenario: Korean A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In Korean, how do you say 'To not be - Polite'? {{c1::아니에요 (anieyo)}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To not be - Polite' en Korean? 아니에요 (anieyo)
content.usage: Term: 아니에요 (anieyo) | Meaning: To not be - Polite

content.text: In Korean, the polite/humble pronoun for 'I' is '{{c1::저}}' (Jeo) and the informal is '{{c2::나}}' (Na).
content.explanation: Use '저' when speaking to elders, strangers, or in professional settings. Use '나' with close friends or younger people.
content.spanish: En coreano, el pronombre para 'yo' es '저' (Jeo - formal/humilde) y '나' (Na - informal).
content.usage: Polite: 저는 학생입니다. (I am a student.) | Informal: 나는 괜찮아. (I am okay.)

content.text: In Korean, the pronoun for 'we / us' is '{{c1::우리}}' (Uri) or politely '{{c2::저희}}' (Jeohui).
content.explanation: Korean culture emphasizes collectivism, so '우리' is often used where English uses 'my' (e.g. 'our wife', 'our house', 'our country').
content.spanish: En coreano, el pronombre para 'nosotros' es '우리' (Uri - común) y '저희' (Jeohui - formal/humilde).
content.usage: Example: 우리 나라 (Our country / my country) | 우리 집 (Our house / my house)

content.text: In Korean, what is the word for 'you (singular)'? Grammatically it is '{{c1::당신}}' (Dangsin) or '{{c2::너}}' (Neo - informal), but in practice, speakers address others by name + title or omit it entirely.
content.explanation: 'Dangsin' can sound confrontational (used in arguments) or intimate (used between spouses). Avoid using it with strangers. 'Neo' is strictly for close friends/kids.
content.spanish: En coreano, 'tú' es '너' (Neo - muy informal). '당신' (Dangsin) se limita a esposos o peleas. Suele omitirse.
content.usage: Rule: Omit 'you' or use title/name + 씨 (ssi). Example: 지수 씨는 뭐 해요? (What are you doing, Jisoo?)

content.text: In Korean, the verb 'to be' (identify Noun A as Noun B) is the copula '{{c1::이다}}' (ida). In formal polite style, it is conjugated as '{{c2::입니다}}' (ibnida).
content.explanation: '입니다' is appended directly to the noun without a space.
content.spanish: En coreano, el verbo 'ser' es la cópula '이다' (ida). En estilo formal cortés se conjuga como '입니다' (ibnida).
content.usage: Example: 학생입니다. (I am a student. [학생 = student])

content.text: In Korean, in informal polite style (해요체), the copula '이다' is conjugated as '{{c1::이에요}}' after a consonant, and '{{c2::예요}}' after a vowel.
content.explanation: Matches phonetics. 예요 is faster to pronounce after a vowel.
content.spanish: En estilo informal cortés, 'ser' se conjuga como '이에요' (tras consonante) y '예요' (tras vocal).
content.usage: Consonant end: 학생이에요. (I am a student.) | Vowel end: 의사예요. (I am a doctor.)

content.text: In Korean, the negative copula 'not to be' is '{{c1::아니다}}' (anida). In formal polite style, it is conjugated as '{{c2::아닙니다}}' (anibnida).
content.explanation: Unlike '입니다', '아닙니다' is written with a space after the noun, which takes the subject marker '이/가'.
content.spanish: El verbo 'no ser' en coreano es '아니다' (anida). En tono formal se conjuga como '아닙니다' (anibnida).
content.usage: Example: 저는 학생이 아닙니다. (I am not a student.)

content.text: In Korean, in informal polite style, the negative copula '아니다' is conjugated as '{{c1::아니에요}}' (anieyo).
content.explanation: Appended after the noun + subject marker.
content.spanish: En tono informal cortés, el verbo 'no ser' se conjuga como '아니에요' (anieyo).
content.usage: Example: 저는 의사가 아니에요. (I am not a doctor.)

content.text: In Korean, the topic marker particles are '{{c1::은}}' (after consonant) and '{{c2::는}}' (after vowel). Example: I (topic) = 저{{c2::는}}.
content.explanation: Used to introduce a topic, create contrast, or state general facts.
content.spanish: Las partículas marcadoras de tema en coreano son '은' (tras consonante) y '는' (tras vocal).
content.usage: Consonant: 이름은 | Vowel: 저는

content.text: In Korean, the subject marker particles are '{{c1::이}}' (after consonant) and '{{c2::가}}' (after vowel). Example: Name (subject) = 이름{{c1::이}}.
content.explanation: Focuses on the subject of the verb, often answering 'who/which' specifically.
content.spanish: Las partículas marcadoras de sujeto en coreano son '이' (tras consonante) y '가' (tras vocal).
content.usage: Consonant: 이름이 | Vowel: 의사가 (doctor)

content.text: In Korean, the object marker particles are '{{c1::을}}' (after consonant) and '{{c2::를}}' (after vowel). Example: Water (object) = 물{{c1::을}}.
content.explanation: Marks the noun that receives the action of a transitive verb.
content.spanish: Las partículas marcadoras de objeto directo en coreano son '을' (tras consonante) y '를' (tras vocal).
content.usage: Consonant: 물을 마시다 (drink water) | Vowel: 커피를 마시다 (drink coffee)

content.text: In Korean, the possessive particle meaning 'of' or equivalent to 's is '{{c1::의}}' (pronounced 'ui' or usually 'e' in spoken language).
content.explanation: Connects Noun A (owner) to Noun B (owned). '저의' (my) is often contracted to '제' (je).
content.spanish: La partícula posesiva en coreano es '의' (se pronuncia 'e' en el habla común). '저의' (mío) se abrevia '제'.
content.usage: Example: 선생님의 책 (The teacher's book) | 저의 -> 제

content.text: In Korean, the demonstrative modifiers for 'this', 'that (near listener)', and 'that (far from both)' are '{{c1::이}}' (i), '{{c2::그}}' (geu), and '{{c3::저}}' (jeo).
content.explanation: These are always placed before a noun. To make them pronouns, combine with '것' (geot - thing): 이것 (this thing), 그것 (that thing), 저것 (that thing over there).
content.spanish: Modificadores demostrativos en coreano: '이' (este), '그' (ese), '저' (aquel).
content.usage: Modifiers: 이 책 (this book) | Pronouns: 이것은 무엇입니까? (What is this?)

content.text: In Korean, the particle that means 'also' or 'too' is '{{c1::도}}' (do). It replaces topic/subject/object particles.
content.explanation: Identical in meaning to Spanish 'también'.
content.spanish: En coreano, la partícula que significa 'también' es '도' (do). Reemplaza a 'eun/neun' o 'i/ga'.
content.usage: Example: 저도 학생이에요. (I am also a student.)

content.text: In Korean, the question word for 'What' is '{{c1::무엇}}' (mueot), which is often contracted to '{{c2::뭐}}' (mwo) in speech.
content.explanation: '뭐' is the standard conversational form.
content.spanish: En coreano, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Qué?' es '무엇' (mueot), que se reduce a '뭐' (mwo) en el habla común.
content.usage: Example: 이것은 무엇입니까? (What is this? - formal) | 이게 뭐야? (What is this? - casual)

content.text: In Korean, the question word for 'Who' is '{{c1::누구}}' (nugu), which becomes '{{c2::누가}}' (nuga) when followed by the subject particle 'ga'.
content.explanation: '누가' is a contraction of '누구' + '가'.
content.spanish: En coreano, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Quién?' es '누구' (nugu), que cambia a '누가' (nuga) al ser sujeto.
content.usage: Example: 누구예요? (Who is it?) | 누가 왔어요? (Who came?)

content.text: In formal polite style, questions are formed by changing the verb ending from '-da' (다) to '{{c1::까}}' (kka) or '{{c2::오}}' (o). For example: 입니다 -> {{c3::입니까?}}.
content.explanation: The formal style uses distinct declarative (-da) and interrogative (-kka) endings.
content.spanish: En estilo formal en coreano, las preguntas terminan en '-까' (-kka) en lugar de '-다' (-da). '입니다' -> '입니까?'.
content.usage: Dialogue: - 학생입니까? - 네, 학생입니다. (- Are you a student? - Yes, I am a student.)
